nodes.name: Unreal Header Tool and Generated Code
nodes.summary: UHT parses supported Unreal declarations and generates support code before normal C++ compilation.
nodes.why_it_matters: It explains reflection syntax, generated-header rules, and a major class of build errors.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Confirmed against UE5.6 documentation.

common_bugs: Fixing generated output instead of source declaration
hands_on_tasks: Create and repair three intentional UHT declaration errors
interview_questions: What does UHT do, and when does it run?

nodes.name: Unreal Reflection System
nodes.summary: Engine-visible metadata and generated support for selected classes, structs, enums, properties, and functions.
nodes.why_it_matters: It underpins editor integration, Blueprint exposure, serialisation, replication metadata, and UObject reference discovery.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Confirmed against UE5.6 documentation.

common_bugs: Confusing metadata with runtime game state
hands_on_tasks: Expose one field separately to editor, Blueprint, save, and replication systems
interview_questions: Why does Unreal need reflection on top of C++?
What does UPROPERTY do besides editor exposure?

nodes.name: Actor and Component Model
nodes.summary: Actors provide independent world identity; Components compose transform-free, spatial, or primitive capabilities into Actors.
nodes.why_it_matters: Correct composition and registration boundaries underpin gameplay, rendering, collision, and replication architecture.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Confirmed against UE5.6 Actor and Component documentation.

hands_on_tasks: Build a reusable health component and attach it to a Character and destructible prop
interview_questions: Actor Component versus Scene Component versus Primitive Component?

nodes.name: Pawn, Character, Controller, and Possession
nodes.summary: Pawns are possessable world agents, Characters add humanoid movement conventions, and Controllers represent player or AI decision sources.
nodes.why_it_matters: The separation enables respawn, spectating, vehicle changes, AI/human reuse, and correct network responsibility placement.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Confirmed against UE5.6 gameplay-framework documentation.

common_bugs: Player identity stored only on Pawn
Input and avatar capability tightly coupled
hands_on_tasks: Implement death, spectating, respawn, and AI possession of a shared Pawn capability

nodes.name: GameMode, GameState, PlayerController, and PlayerState
nodes.summary: Framework classes divide server-only rules, shared match state, owning-player control, and broadly replicated participant state.
nodes.why_it_matters: Lifetime and network audience mistakes commonly work standalone and fail in multiplayer or across respawn.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Confirmed against UE5.6 framework and APlayerState API documentation.

common_bugs: Participant identity stored only on Pawn
Secret state broadly replicated in PlayerState
hands_on_tasks: Replicate team score through GameState and preserve player score through Pawn respawn
source_conflicts: High-level state examples require audience/privacy qualification.

nodes.name: Actor and Component Lifecycle
nodes.summary: Loaded, duplicated, immediate-spawned, and deferred-spawned Actors follow distinct early paths before component initialisation and play.
nodes.why_it_matters: Wrong-hook code produces CDO/editor side effects, duplicate bindings, unregistered components, and cleanup leaks.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Broad model confirmed; detailed sequence source is UE4.27-era and exact UE5 ordering is version-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Trace placed, PIE-duplicated, immediate-spawned, and deferred-spawned Actors
interview_questions: Why use EndPlay for gameplay cleanup?
source_conflicts: UE4.27 lifecycle sequence requires UE5 source/API confirmation for exact hook ordering.

nodes.name: Subsystem Lifetimes and Service Placement
nodes.summary: Subsystems are auto-instanced services scoped to Engine, Editor, GameInstance, World, or LocalPlayer hosts.
nodes.why_it_matters: Choosing the narrowest correct host avoids global-state leaks and supports multi-world and multi-player correctness.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Target-era API confirms supported host lifetimes.

common_bugs: World state placed in GameInstance subsystem
hands_on_tasks: Implement a WorldSubsystem registry and prove isolation across PIE worlds
interview_questions: When should logic live in a subsystem?

nodes.name: UObject Model
nodes.summary: Unreal-managed object identity, type data, engine services, and garbage-collected lifetime layered on C++.
nodes.why_it_matters: Most gameplay and editor architecture rests on choosing correctly between UObject, Actor, reflected value, and ordinary C++ type.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Core model stable; UE5 pointer idioms evolved.

common_bugs: Making hot value data into UObjects without need
hands_on_tasks: Compare a plain struct, USTRUCT value, UObject, and Actor for one data model
interview_questions: When should a type be a UObject rather than a plain C++ type?

nodes.name: Class Default Object and Construction
nodes.summary: Each UClass has a default template object; constructors establish defaults and default subobjects.
nodes.why_it_matters: It prevents world-dependent constructor bugs and explains default propagation and Blueprint class defaults.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Confirmed against UE5.6 documentation.

common_bugs: Runtime delegate binding on the CDO
hands_on_tasks: Log constructor, PostInitProperties, and BeginPlay for template and instances
interview_questions: What is the CDO and why does it change constructor design?

nodes.name: UObject Garbage Collection
nodes.summary: Mark-and-sweep reclamation based on reachability through roots and discoverable strong references.
nodes.why_it_matters: Incorrect reference graphs cause disappearing objects, unintended retention, and frame hitches.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Mark/sweep is baseline; incremental reachability is Experimental in UE5.6.

common_bugs: Assuming C++ scope controls UObject destruction
debugging_tools: Incremental GC verification cvars when applicable
hands_on_tasks: Predict and observe collection for strong, weak, soft, raw, and rooted references
interview_questions: How does Unreal decide a UObject is collectable?
How would you debug a UObject that disappears?

nodes.name: UObject Pointer Selection
nodes.summary: Choose raw, TObjectPtr, weak, soft, or strong references from retention and loading intent.
nodes.why_it_matters: The wrong pointer expresses the wrong lifetime/dependency contract even when code appears to work.

version_notes.label: Changed in UE5
version_notes.details: Prefer reflected TObjectPtr fields; recognise historical raw UPROPERTY pointers. TLazyObjectPtr is deprecated.

common_bugs: Soft path treated as loaded object
hands_on_tasks: Implement a five-case pointer matrix and record observed GC/loading behaviour
interview_questions: Why should persistent UObject members usually be UPROPERTY TObjectPtr?
source_conflicts: Broad smart-pointer wording in SRC-EPIC-003 is refined by the dedicated SRC-EPIC-009 pointer guide.

nodes.name: C++ Object Lifetime and Storage
nodes.summary: Objects have typed lifetimes nested within storage duration; surviving addresses and handles can dangle after lifetime ends.
nodes.why_it_matters: Use-after-free, dangling captures, shutdown bugs, and invalid views require reasoning beyond null checks.

version_notes.label: Standard C++
version_notes.details: Core concept; curriculum examples target C++17+.

hands_on_tasks: Trigger and diagnose a local dangling pointer and a shutdown-order bug

nodes.name: RAII and Rule of Zero/Five
nodes.summary: Bind resources to object lifetime and compose self-managing members; make every special-member operation deliberate when custom ownership is required.
nodes.why_it_matters: It makes cleanup structural and prevents leaks, double release, shallow-copy ownership, and silently suppressed moves.

version_notes.label: Standard C++
version_notes.details: Rule of Five/Zero applies to C++11+; Rule of Zero preferred.

hands_on_tasks: Build a move-only RAII registration and prove exactly one release
interview_questions: Explain Rule of Zero and Rule of Five.
source_conflicts: Rule of Five is often oversimplified as hand-writing five functions; Rule of Zero and explicit default/delete are preferred.

nodes.name: Copy, Move, and Value Return
nodes.summary: Copy preserves logical value; move enables representation transfer; modern return-by-value relies on elision and implicit move.
nodes.why_it_matters: Correct semantics affect class safety, container compatibility, API clarity, and performance.

version_notes.label: Standard C++
version_notes.details: Move is C++11+; guaranteed same-type prvalue construction is C++17.

hands_on_tasks: Instrument factory return and vector relocation with throwing and noexcept moves

nodes.name: Unique, Shared, Weak, and Borrowed Ownership
nodes.summary: Direct values and unique ownership are defaults; shared ownership is explicit co-lifetime, weak edges observe, and raw/reference handles borrow.
nodes.why_it_matters: Ownership ambiguity causes leaks, cycles, premature destruction, unexpected thread destruction, and hot-path overhead.

version_notes.label: Standard C++ and UE5
version_notes.details: Standard C++11+ and UE5.6 smart-pointer APIs.

common_bugs: Smart pointer passed when only borrowing
Reference count mistaken for pointee synchronisation
hands_on_tasks: Refactor a shared ownership web into one owner plus borrowers and one intentional weak edge

nodes.name: Iterator, Pointer, and Reference Invalidation
nodes.summary: Container mutation can invalidate element handles even while the container remains alive.
nodes.why_it_matters: Reallocation and erase-related dangling handles are common, intermittent game bugs.

version_notes.label: Standard C++
version_notes.details: std::vector rules cited; UE container rules require separate confirmation.

hands_on_tasks: Predict and verify handle validity across reserve, push, insert, and erase

nodes.name: Standard C++ versus UObject Lifetime Domains
nodes.summary: RAII/reference-counted ordinary objects and reachability-collected UObjects use different ownership and observation mechanisms.
nodes.why_it_matters: Pointer names look similar, but crossing domains incorrectly creates leaks, invalid references, and false thread-safety assumptions.

version_notes.label: Changed in UE5
version_notes.details: UE5 TObjectPtr preference; non-UObject shared pointer domain remains distinct.

common_bugs: GC semantics assumed for ordinary C++ object
hands_on_tasks: Run standard ownership and UObject reachability experiments side by side
interview_questions: Compare shared_ptr, TSharedPtr, and UObject pointers.

nodes.name: FName, FString, and FText
nodes.summary: Choose identifier, mutable character data, or localisable display text semantics deliberately.
nodes.why_it_matters: Wrong choices create localisation loss, conversion churn, unclear identity, and poor APIs.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Confirmed against target-era UE5 string documentation.

hands_on_tasks: Audit string fields and justify every type and conversion

nodes.name: TArray, TSet, and TMap
nodes.summary: Unreal value-owning sequence and hash containers with engine reflection/serialisation integration and distinct order/invalidation contracts.
nodes.why_it_matters: Container choice controls access complexity, locality, ordering, reflection support, and handle validity.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Core semantics stable; exact traits/APIs are version-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Compare TArray, TSet, and TMap for a representative registry
source_conflicts: Do not infer UE invalidation/relocation rules solely from standard containers.

nodes.name: UObject/Actor Creation and Runtime Casts
nodes.summary: Creation APIs select UObject, Actor, deferred-spawn, or class-default-subobject lifecycle; Cast performs checked UObject conversion.
nodes.why_it_matters: Wrong APIs bypass lifecycle contracts, while repeated type discovery creates architectural coupling.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Core APIs stable; exact lifecycle order remains version-sensitive.

common_bugs: Cast chain used as capability system
hands_on_tasks: Trace NewObject, SpawnActor, deferred spawn, and default subobject creation

nodes.name: Native, Multicast, and Dynamic Delegates
nodes.summary: Select callback cardinality, reflection capability, and lifetime-aware binding independently.
nodes.why_it_matters: Delegate misuse causes dangling callbacks, duplicate invocation, hidden reflection cost, and lifecycle leaks.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Core categories stable; macro overload sets can change.

common_bugs: Dynamic delegate used in native hot path
hands_on_tasks: Implement native and Blueprint-assignable health events and destroy listeners safely
interview_questions: Native versus multicast versus dynamic delegate?

nodes.name: C++ and Blueprint API Boundary
nodes.summary: Keep stable invariants and authority in narrow native APIs while exposing designer-owned data, composition, and bounded extension to Blueprint.
nodes.why_it_matters: A deliberate boundary improves iteration, testability, authority, performance visibility, and packaged-build reliability.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Specifier set confirmed against UE5.6 exposure documentation.

common_bugs: Mandatory invariant depends on Call Parent
hands_on_tasks: Refactor health into native invariants, read-only state, event-driven UI, and Blueprint presentation hook
interview_questions: What belongs in C++ versus Blueprint?
source_conflicts: Performance is workload-specific; no universal C++/Blueprint speed ratio is asserted.

nodes.name: Network Authority, Roles, and Owning Connection
nodes.summary: Server authority owns replicated truth; roles describe local simulation/control; Actor ownership maps RPC and owner policies to a connection.
nodes.why_it_matters: Confusing these relationships causes rejected RPCs, insecure validation, and listen-host-only behaviour.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Core client/server model stable; exact role/RPC tables version-sensitive.

common_bugs: Client calls RPC on unowned Actor
hands_on_tasks: Trace authority, role, and ownership for server, owner client, and remote client
interview_questions: Why does a Server RPC fail?
source_conflicts: Autonomous proxy language must not be interpreted as authoritative replicated truth.

nodes.name: Replicated Properties, OnRep, and Conditions
nodes.summary: Server state converges to selected clients through registered replicated properties, optional RepNotify reactions, and audience conditions.
nodes.why_it_matters: It is the baseline mechanism for durable state, late joiners, UI reaction, and bandwidth/privacy policy.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Generic replication baseline; macro/options can evolve.

hands_on_tasks: Replicate health and one coherent match-state struct with owner/public conditions

nodes.name: Server, Client, and Multicast RPCs
nodes.summary: Directed remote calls carry player intent, targeted responses, or transient fan-out under ownership, relevance, and reliability rules.
nodes.why_it_matters: Wrong routing/reliability produces dropped calls, insecurity, backlog latency, and late-join inconsistency.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Core types stable; exact execution/order details version-sensitive.

common_bugs: Client multicast expected to fan out
hands_on_tasks: Implement validated Server intent, targeted Client response, and unreliable cosmetic multicast

nodes.name: Actor Relevancy and Network Dormancy
nodes.summary: Relevancy filters Actors per connection; dormancy skips stable Actors while retaining their client instances.
nodes.why_it_matters: These policies dominate scaling and explain missing/destroyed proxies and lost dormant updates.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Core concepts stable; partial dormancy/deprecation and implementation details version-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Contrast a relevance-limited dynamic pickup with a dormant persistent objective
source_conflicts: After-mutation dormancy flush may appear to work but is implementation-dependent.

nodes.name: Character Movement Prediction and Reconciliation
nodes.summary: Autonomous clients predict and save moves; the server reproduces/validates; clients correct and replay; simulated proxies smooth updates.
nodes.why_it_matters: It explains responsive movement, corrections, and why direct transform manipulation fights built-in networking.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Architecture stable; packed move APIs and internals evolve.

common_bugs: Custom state absent from saved move
hands_on_tasks: Trigger a correction and explain saved move, server check, correction, replay, and smoothing

nodes.name: Custom CharacterMovement Saved Moves
nodes.summary: Movement-affecting custom state must be captured, compacted, restored, combined safely and validated so client prediction and server reproduction agree.
nodes.why_it_matters: Predicted sprint, dash, slide and wall-run bugs often come from changing movement state outside the saved-move protocol.

version_notes.label: UE5.3-UE5.6 target, API-sensitive
version_notes.details: Saved-move virtuals, compressed flags and packed move pathways require target header/source verification.

common_bugs: Replicated bool instead of saved move
Replay does not restore custom state
hands_on_tasks: Project 3 predicted sprint and dash saved-move extension
source_conflicts: Epic API pages are anchors; exact signatures must be checked in target source.

nodes.name: Lag Compensation and Server Rewind
nodes.summary: Store bounded historical hit-relevant server snapshots so authoritative action validation can account for what a client plausibly saw.
nodes.why_it_matters: Responsive shooting and melee require fairness policy, server authority, memory budgeting and abuse resistance under latency.

version_notes.label: Game-specific synthesis
version_notes.details: Server rewind design is not treated as one generic UE subsystem; target projects define windows, hitboxes, blockers and security policy.

hands_on_tasks: Project 3 bounded hitscan rewind buffer with fairness memo
source_conflicts: Policy and implementation are genre/project-specific.

nodes.name: Prediction, Reconciliation, Rewind, and Rollback Terminology
nodes.summary: Use precise terms for local prediction, server correction/replay, remote smoothing, server rewind and full rollback resimulation.
nodes.why_it_matters: Interview and design discussions become confused when CharacterMovement correction, lag compensation and deterministic rollback are all called rollback.

version_notes.label: Conceptual; implementation-sensitive
version_notes.details: Project-specific Network Prediction plugin or custom rollback systems must be labelled separately.

hands_on_tasks: Draw separate timelines for movement correction and lag-compensated shot

nodes.name: NetworkPrediction Plugin Branch Proof
nodes.summary: Verify target-branch plugin status, headers, model APIs, services and debug CVars before implementing rollback with Epic's NetworkPrediction plugin.
nodes.why_it_matters: Maintained public API pages prove vocabulary but not exact UE5.3-UE5.6 integration signatures or production support.

version_notes.label: Plugin-dependent and source-sensitive
version_notes.details: Public API page observed as maintained UE5.8 during 2026-06 lookup; UE5.3-UE5.6 targets require source/header verification.

common_bugs: Copying maintained-doc signatures into older target branch
hands_on_tasks: Project 3 NetworkPrediction rollback proof extension Lab 1
interview_questions: Does NetworkPrediction mean automatic full-game rollback?
What proof is needed before using the plugin?
source_conflicts: Maintained API page does not prove target minor-version integration.

nodes.name: Rollback Input, Sync, and Aux State Model
nodes.summary: Design compact replayable input commands, authoritative sync state and versioned aux state for a bounded rollback simulation.
nodes.why_it_matters: Missing or contaminated state causes false convergence, reconcile spam, exploit acceptance and replay with wrong tunables.

version_notes.label: Conceptual with branch-specific APIs
version_notes.details: State categories are architectural; exact NetworkPrediction templates/serialisation helpers need target source.

common_bugs: Aux state read from current Actor
Old frames replay with new equipment tuning
hands_on_tasks: Rollback dash input/sync/aux state lab

nodes.name: Rollback Cues and Side-Effect Safety
nodes.summary: Classify replay-safe cues separately from irreversible authority transactions so prediction and resimulation do not duplicate effects.
nodes.why_it_matters: Rollback bugs often appear as duplicated VFX, camera shakes, damage, rewards or analytics when replay runs the same tick twice.

version_notes.label: Plugin-dependent
version_notes.details: Public API page proves cue trait vocabulary; exact trait implementation and registration are target-branch details.

common_bugs: Authority cue used for local-only feedback
hands_on_tasks: Cue trait and dedupe matrix lab
interview_questions: Why are rollback side effects dangerous?
How do cues differ from gameplay transactions?

nodes.name: Rollback Reconcile Proof and Profiling
nodes.summary: Force divergence, prove restore/replay/convergence, then measure correction frequency, replay CPU, buffer memory and network payloads.
nodes.why_it_matters: A rollback prototype is not production evidence unless it survives packet conditions, forced correction, side-effect checks and budget measurement.

version_notes.label: Tooling and CVar sensitive
version_notes.details: Force/print reconcile CVars and smoothing controls must be queried in the exact target build.

hands_on_tasks: Rollback dash forced reconcile proof and cost report
interview_questions: What proof would convince you to ship rollback?

nodes.name: Network Profiling and Replication Scalability
nodes.summary: Measure traffic and server replication cost, then optimise audiences, frequency, state representation, dormancy, and routing architecture.
nodes.why_it_matters: Bandwidth and per-connection Actor consideration grow into latency and server frame failures at scale.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Tooling and Iris/RepGraph support/configuration are version-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Capture before/after owner conditions and dormancy under identical multi-client load
interview_questions: When consider Replication Graph or Iris?
source_conflicts: Network Profiler is legacy in current docs; exact preferred tool depends on target build.

nodes.name: AIController and Decision Architecture
nodes.summary: Separate controller decision policy from Pawn physical capabilities and decompose sensing, memory, decisions, queries, paths, and steering.
nodes.why_it_matters: Layer separation makes systems reusable and lets failures be localised instead of blamed on the whole AI.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: AIController separation stable; decision technologies vary.

common_bugs: AI running on wrong network side
hands_on_tasks: Drive one Pawn capability from PlayerController and AIController

nodes.name: Behaviour Tree, Blackboard, and Observer Aborts
nodes.summary: Event-driven priority/action selection using Blackboard memory, tasks, decorators, services, and abort-aware branch changes.
nodes.why_it_matters: Correct node/lifecycle design gives responsive, debuggable AI without per-frame tree polling.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Core model stable; node details version-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Build Patrol/Combat priority branches with acquisition/loss observer aborts

nodes.name: StateTree, Behaviour Tree, and FSM Trade-offs
nodes.summary: Choose reactive priority trees, hierarchical state/transition selection, or small explicit state machines according to semantics and tooling.
nodes.why_it_matters: Fashion-driven decision frameworks create avoidable complexity and migration cost.

version_notes.label: UE5-specific
version_notes.details: StateTree is UE5-era and plugin/module/version sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Model patrol/chase/attack in BT, StateTree, and FSM and defend one
interview_questions: Behaviour Tree versus StateTree versus FSM?
source_conflicts: No official universal replacement guidance; choice is architectural.

nodes.name: StateTree Execution and Data Flow
nodes.summary: StateTree selects root-to-leaf active paths, runs active-state tasks, evaluates transitions and binds parameters, context data, evaluators and task output deliberately.
nodes.why_it_matters: Most StateTree failures look like graph logic but actually come from wrong context, premature task completion, missing cleanup or misunderstood transition hierarchy.

version_notes.label: UE5 plugin/project-sensitive
version_notes.details: StateTree extension classes, schema/data-view APIs and debugger UI need target-branch verification.

common_bugs: Helper task completes before main action
hands_on_tasks: Build Acquire/Reach/Use/Release StateTree and capture transition reasons

nodes.name: Smart Object Affordances, Definitions, and Slots
nodes.summary: Represent world opportunities as authored definitions/components with slots, activity tags, user requirements and behavior references.
nodes.why_it_matters: Reusable interactions collapse into brittle Actor-specific scripts unless the affordance and slot contract is explicit.

version_notes.label: UE5 plugin/project-sensitive
version_notes.details: Smart Object Definition, Component and Slot API fields require target-header verification.

common_bugs: Runtime mutable state stored in authoring data
hands_on_tasks: Author chair, cover and terminal Smart Objects with distinct slots and requirements
interview_questions: What problem do Smart Objects solve?

nodes.name: Smart Object Query, Claim, Use, and Release Lifecycle
nodes.summary: Separate candidate discovery from slot claim, movement/reach validation, behavior execution and release on all success/failure/abort paths.
nodes.why_it_matters: Race conditions and leaked claims are the dominant Smart Object production bugs.

version_notes.label: UE5 plugin/project-sensitive
version_notes.details: Subsystem request/claim/use/release method names and handle semantics are API-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Run two agents racing for one slot and prove release on five failure paths
interview_questions: How debug two NPCs using one slot?

nodes.name: Smart Object Authority, Debugging, and Scaling
nodes.summary: Validate durable interaction claims on authority, debug lifecycle phases separately and profile query/path/StateTree work under representative agent counts.
nodes.why_it_matters: Smart Object systems often fail at multiplayer validation or authored scale rather than at single-object happy paths.

version_notes.label: UE5 plugin/project-sensitive
version_notes.details: Replication policy, Mass integration and debugger categories must be proven in target project.

hands_on_tasks: Profile synchronous versus staggered Smart Object queries at 1/20/100 agents
interview_questions: How should Smart Object interaction work in multiplayer?
How profile Smart Object activity systems?

nodes.name: AI Perception and Stimulus Memory
nodes.summary: Configured senses receive stimuli events; explicit memory policy distinguishes currently perceived, known, last-known, and forgotten state.
nodes.why_it_matters: Incorrect stimulus lifecycle creates psychic AI, instant forgetting, and decision churn.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: UE5.6 Perception docs; affiliation details version-sensitive.

common_bugs: Clear target on any lost-sight event
hands_on_tasks: Implement sight/hearing memory and timed investigation/forgetting
interview_questions: How should Perception memory be handled?

nodes.name: Custom AI Sense Pipeline
nodes.summary: Design custom perception channels as batched evidence producers that emit stimuli without owning final decisions.
nodes.why_it_matters: Custom senses easily become hidden gameplay state machines or hot-loop trace floods unless source, sense, memory and decision responsibilities are separated.

version_notes.label: UE5.3-UE5.6 source/API-sensitive
version_notes.details: UAISense, UAISenseConfig, FAIStimulus and UAIPerceptionComponent signatures must be verified in target headers.

common_bugs: Stale Actor source used after destruction
hands_on_tasks: Implement or sketch a scent sense with event schema, config, memory projection and six registration/filter failures
interview_questions: When implement a custom AI sense?
How debug custom sense never reports?
What belongs in a custom stimulus?
source_conflicts: Official API pages are dynamically rendered and maintained; implementation examples remain schematic until source-verified.

nodes.name: Perception Memory Projection and Evidence Decay
nodes.summary: Merge sight, hearing, damage and custom stimuli into explicit current, known, last-evidence and confidence state with decay.
nodes.why_it_matters: Most stealth/investigation bugs come from treating one stimulus callback as the whole target truth.

version_notes.label: Stable architecture; config/API-sensitive
version_notes.details: Per-sense Max Age and callback helpers require target configuration checks.

common_bugs: Old streamed evidence rehydrates as fresh
hands_on_tasks: Build see, lose, hear, scent decay, investigate, forget and reacquire sequence with logged memory state
interview_questions: Why does a guard remember forever?
Why should custom sense not write Blackboard keys directly?

nodes.name: Environment Query System
nodes.summary: Generators create candidates, Contexts provide reference frames, and Tests filter/score environmental choices.
nodes.why_it_matters: EQS makes spatial decisions data-driven but can become trace/path-query expensive at scale.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Plugin/configuration status should be verified.

hands_on_tasks: Build and profile a reachable line-of-sight attack-position query

nodes.name: NavMesh, MoveTo, Path Following, and Avoidance
nodes.summary: NavMesh graph search computes routes; path following executes them; RVO or Detour locally avoids dynamic agents.
nodes.why_it_matters: MoveTo failures and crowd problems span possession, nav data, agent config, requests, movement, and steering layers.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Core NavMesh concepts stable; avoidance/API details target-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Create six MoveTo failures and compare no avoidance/RVO/Detour

nodes.name: Frame Budgets and Bottleneck Classification
nodes.summary: Define frame-time targets and classify Game, Draw, RHI, GPU, memory, I/O, network, or display constraints before detailed analysis.
nodes.why_it_matters: Optimising the wrong lane wastes time and can regress other platforms or quality.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Concept stable; stat/tool UI evolves.

hands_on_tasks: Classify independent game-thread and GPU workloads from blind captures
interview_questions: What does stat unit tell you?

nodes.name: Timing Insights and Scoped Instrumentation
nodes.summary: Capture aligned thread/task/GPU traces, compare frames, inspect scopes/callers/counts, and add targeted project instrumentation.
nodes.why_it_matters: It turns a high-level bottleneck into a causal call path and measurable intervention.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Trace channels/panels vary by minor version.

common_bugs: Total time sorted without call count
hands_on_tasks: Find duplicate scheduling and verify removal with a before/after trace

nodes.name: Memory Insights, Allocation Churn, and GC
nodes.summary: Investigate capacity, growth, short/long-lived allocation churn, retention and GC spikes across repeatable lifecycle markers.
nodes.why_it_matters: Memory bugs cause crashes, hitches, fragmentation, streaming churn, and hidden CPU cost.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Memory trace capabilities/platform callstacks vary.

common_bugs: GC interval tuned before root cause
hands_on_tasks: Identify native retention, short-lived churn, and a GC spike across repeated cycles
source_conflicts: Manual GC/pooling are conditional interventions, not defaults.

nodes.name: Gameplay CPU Scheduling and Data Optimisation
nodes.summary: Optimise measured frequency, algorithms, allocation, locality, duplication and synchronisation using Tick/events/timers/batching appropriately.
nodes.why_it_matters: Most gameplay CPU regressions are multiplicative count/frequency/data problems rather than one slow function.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Specific scheduling APIs evolve; workflow stable.

hands_on_tasks: Compare polling, events, timers and dirty batching under low/high change rates
interview_questions: Tick, timer, callback or dirty batching?
source_conflicts: Event-driven updates are not universally cheaper than batched updates.

nodes.name: Scalability and Device Profiles
nodes.summary: Expose quality/cost tiers and platform/device-family configuration while preserving gameplay correctness and readability.
nodes.why_it_matters: The bottleneck and memory envelope move across hardware; one developer-PC configuration cannot serve every target.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Exact CVars/groups/selectors are platform/version-sensitive.

common_bugs: Scalability used to excuse baseline waste
hands_on_tasks: Create and validate three quality tiers plus two device profiles
interview_questions: How do scalability and device profiles differ from optimisation?

nodes.name: Target Benchmark Capture Contract
nodes.summary: Define build identity, target hardware, quality/profile settings, scenario, warm-up/window, tools, metrics and pass/fail rule before comparing performance.
nodes.why_it_matters: Most optimisation claims fail because the workload or environment changed rather than the code becoming faster.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred; target-sensitive
version_notes.details: Trace channels, device profile selection and platform profiler setup must be checked in the target branch/device.

hands_on_tasks: Write and execute a fixed camera packaged benchmark contract under two profiles

nodes.name: Memory Insights and LLM Budget Workflow
nodes.summary: Use LLM/tag checkpoints for ownership and Memory Insights for allocation lifetime, growth, churn and callstack diagnosis.
nodes.why_it_matters: A memory fix depends on whether the issue is capacity, growth, churn, streaming pressure, UObject retention or locality.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred; platform/build-config sensitive
version_notes.details: Memory trace callstacks, LLM tag coverage and overhead vary by platform/configuration.

common_bugs: Global pool raised before ownership proof
hands_on_tasks: Compare UI open/close growth using both LLM tag ownership and Memory Insights callstacks
source_conflicts: Memory fixes are platform/content/workload dependent; no single pool/churn policy is universal.

nodes.name: CSV Performance Regression Gates
nodes.summary: Use lightweight CSV telemetry and automation scenarios to detect drift, then escalate failures to targeted causal captures.
nodes.why_it_matters: Performance CI must catch real regressions without making every change pay full profiler/capture cost or fail on noise.

version_notes.label: UE5.6 target; infrastructure-sensitive
version_notes.details: CSV categories, automation hooks, hardware availability and threshold policy are project-specific.

hands_on_tasks: Create a CSV gate with scenario/window/threshold/tolerance/artifact/owner and one failing regression

nodes.name: Render Pipeline, RHI, and GPU Passes
nodes.summary: Relate overlapping Game/Render/RHI/GPU lanes to view construction and depth, shadow, base/GBuffer, lighting, translucency and post passes.
nodes.why_it_matters: A pass-level frame model is required to localise rendering regressions and communicate across gameplay, engine and art roles.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Pass graph, event names and threading paths vary by platform/features/version.

common_bugs: Draw cost confused with GPU cost
hands_on_tasks: Annotate one target frame from Game lane through major GPU passes
source_conflicts: Maintained Epic pages may describe later UE5 pass/features.

nodes.name: Rendering Cost Dimensions and Pass-led Diagnosis
nodes.summary: Separate CPU submission, geometry, pixel/fill, shader, overdraw and bandwidth pressure and test pass-mechanism hypotheses.
nodes.why_it_matters: Optimisations fail when draw calls, polygons, materials and fill rate are treated as one generic graphics cost.

version_notes.label: Stable principle; UE5.3–UE5.6 tools
version_notes.details: Tool views and pass names vary; mechanism-based workflow is stable.

hands_on_tasks: Build isolated object, geometry, opaque pixel and translucent overdraw workloads

nodes.name: Materials, Shader Permutations, WPO, and Translucency
nodes.summary: Choose dynamic parameters, bounded static variants and surface modes while accounting for runtime shading, overdraw, bounds and build/PSO costs.
nodes.why_it_matters: Materials affect pixel/vertex work, passes, shadows, culling, shader libraries and runtime pipeline diversity.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Substrate and renderer material support are version-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Sweep static permutations and profile opaque/masked/translucent/WPO variants

nodes.name: Culling, Instancing, LOD, HLOD, and Nanite
nodes.summary: Choose a geometry representation by submission, visibility, detail, streaming, material/deformation and target-platform constraints.
nodes.why_it_matters: Modern UE geometry choices trade CPU scene cost, GPU visibility/raster cost, memory, culling granularity and authoring flexibility.

version_notes.label: UE5-specific/version-sensitive
version_notes.details: Nanite support changes substantially between UE5 minor versions; verify UE5.3–UE5.6 branch.

common_bugs: Current support backported to old branch
hands_on_tasks: Compare Components, ISM/HISM, LOD, HLOD and supported Nanite representation
interview_questions: Nanite versus LOD, instancing and HLOD?
source_conflicts: Current maintained Nanite docs include features later than UE5.6.

nodes.name: Lumen GI and Reflection Diagnosis
nodes.summary: Diagnose dynamic GI/reflection quality and cost across screen traces, software/hardware tracing and Lumen scene/surface representations.
nodes.why_it_matters: Lumen artefacts and cost often originate in representation or tracing mode rather than a single generic quality slider.

version_notes.label: UE5-specific/version-sensitive
version_notes.details: Tracing support, quality and visualisations vary by platform and UE5 release.

hands_on_tasks: Create and repair a Lumen scene-representation artefact
interview_questions: How debug a Lumen artefact or cost?
source_conflicts: Performance examples are setting/platform targets, not universal budgets.

nodes.name: Virtual Shadow Map Pages and Invalidation
nodes.summary: Reason about demand-allocated cached shadow pages, new visibility, moving lights/casters, WPO and non-Nanite shadow submission.
nodes.why_it_matters: VSM regressions are often cache-invalidation and affected-page problems rather than only generic shadow quality cost.

version_notes.label: UE5-specific/actively developed
version_notes.details: CVars, caching behaviour, limits and visualisations are minor-version-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Compare static, rigid-moving and WPO shadow casters
interview_questions: How do VSMs cache and invalidate pages?

nodes.name: RDG Passes, Resources, and Dependencies
nodes.summary: Declare deferred render passes and resource access so RDG can derive lifetimes, transitions, culling, aliasing and scheduling.
nodes.why_it_matters: Renderer extensions require correct deferred lifetimes and complete resource contracts before low-level optimisation is safe.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6/source-sensitive
version_notes.details: Exact RDG APIs, validation and Insights capabilities evolve.

hands_on_tasks: Add and inspect a minimal named RDG pass in a source-capable branch
interview_questions: What is RDG and why declare resources?
source_conflicts: Maintained guide may include APIs unavailable in UE5.3–UE5.6.

nodes.name: Shader Permutations and PSO Cache Workflow
nodes.summary: Separate compiled shader variants, runtime shader cost and first-use Pipeline State Object creation/cache coverage.
nodes.why_it_matters: Shader and PSO problems cause build pain, memory/library growth and runtime hitches that require different evidence and fixes.

version_notes.label: RHI/platform/branch-sensitive
version_notes.details: PSO collection, cache formats, precache systems and shader library behaviour require target packaged verification.

common_bugs: Shader availability confused with PSO coverage
hands_on_tasks: Project 4 PSO first-use hitch lab and shader permutation audit
interview_questions: Shader permutation, runtime shader cost or PSO hitch?
source_conflicts: Exact PSO workflow differs across RHIs and platforms.

nodes.name: Global Shader and Plugin Shader Workflow
nodes.summary: Add custom global/plugin shaders with correct source mapping, module registration, permutation predicates, RDG parameters and output validation.
nodes.why_it_matters: Renderer/tool extensions often fail from load order, shader path, permutation, parameter or deferred-lifetime mistakes before the algorithm matters.

version_notes.label: Source/API-sensitive
version_notes.details: Macros, module load phase, shader mapping and RDG helper APIs must be compiled in the target branch.

hands_on_tasks: Project 4 minimal global shader/RDG pass
interview_questions: How build safe custom global shader/RDG pass?
source_conflicts: Implementation docs must be matched to target branch source.

nodes.name: Mesh Drawing Pipeline and Draw Command Pressure
nodes.summary: Understand how primitives become scene proxies, mesh batches and pass-specific cached or dynamic draw commands.
nodes.why_it_matters: Gameplay-side component, material-slot and render-state decisions can create render-thread/Draw/RHI cost before the GPU is the limiter.

version_notes.label: Renderer-internal/source-sensitive
version_notes.details: Exact mesh draw command caches/classes/pass processors evolve across UE5 branches.

hands_on_tasks: Project 4 1000-component versus ISM/HISM/Nanite/dynamic-dirty comparison
interview_questions: What does the mesh drawing pipeline explain?
source_conflicts: Use source for exact internals; curriculum covers mental model and debugging implications.

nodes.name: Render Targets and Scene Capture Budgets
nodes.summary: Budget render targets and scene captures by memory, bandwidth, update cadence, view count, format, mips/copies/readbacks and consumers.
nodes.why_it_matters: Mirrors, minimaps, thumbnails, security cameras and UI previews can secretly add extra views, PSOs, memory and first-use hitches.

version_notes.label: Feature/settings/platform-sensitive
version_notes.details: SceneCapture settings, show flags and renderer paths require target capture proof.

common_bugs: Cube capture treated as one view
hands_on_tasks: Project 4 minimap/thumbnail/security camera/cube capture budget matrix
interview_questions: How budget render targets and scene captures?
source_conflicts: Example scene-capture pages require production setting validation.

nodes.name: RenderDoc and Platform GPU Capture Workflow
nodes.summary: Escalate from Unreal pass timings to external GPU captures when exact pipeline state, resources, shaders or platform behaviour must be inspected.
nodes.why_it_matters: Engine pass timings localise cost, but GPU captures are often required to diagnose binding, shader, occupancy, barrier or RHI/vendor issues.

version_notes.label: Tool/RHI/platform-sensitive
version_notes.details: RenderDoc support and capture setup vary; console/mobile targets may require vendor/platform tools.

common_bugs: Capture used before pass is named
Finding not verified in normal profiler
hands_on_tasks: Project 4 one external GPU capture with event markers and before/after profiler proof
interview_questions: When escalate to RenderDoc/platform GPU tools?
source_conflicts: RenderDoc is not a universal substitute for target platform tools.

nodes.name: Component Contracts and Composition
nodes.summary: Compose coherent capabilities with explicit owner, lifecycle, authority, replication and dependency contracts instead of subclass or cast webs.
nodes.why_it_matters: Composition is Unreal's common reuse unit but hidden sibling/global coupling can recreate a monolith.

version_notes.label: Stable UE4/UE5
version_notes.details: Component registration/replication details remain target-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Refactor combinatorial subclasses and document every new Component contract

nodes.name: Direct Calls, Observers, Commands, and Event Queues
nodes.summary: Select communication by required result, fan-out, timing, durability, ordering, capacity, lifetime and network boundary.
nodes.why_it_matters: Calling every mechanism decoupled hides re-entrancy, missed state, stale listeners, queue overflow and protocol errors.

version_notes.label: Stable patterns; UE5.3–UE5.6 mechanisms
version_notes.details: Delegate binding and message APIs are target-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Compare one transition via call, delegate, Command and bounded queue

nodes.name: State, Strategy, and Policy Selection
nodes.summary: Use State for modes/transitions and Strategy for interchangeable policies, retaining simple branches for small stable variants.
nodes.why_it_matters: Boolean state explosions and giant variation switches produce invalid combinations and unsafe extension points.

version_notes.label: Stable patterns
version_notes.details: UE StateTree is a separate version/plugin-sensitive framework.

common_bugs: Strategy abstraction for two trivial cases
hands_on_tasks: Implement weapon State plus two Strategies and compare a simple branch

nodes.name: Data-driven Definitions, Runtime State, and Presentation
nodes.summary: Separate validated authored definitions with stable asset identity from mutable per-instance state and local presentation/loading.
nodes.why_it_matters: Content scale, save compatibility, replication, loading and designer workflows fail when definitions and runtime state are mixed.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Asset Manager configuration/loading APIs are version/project-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Create item/weapon definitions, runtime records, validation and missing-content migration

nodes.name: Subsystem and Service Boundaries
nodes.summary: Choose a scoped subsystem host deliberately and keep retrieval near composition boundaries when hidden dependencies harm testing or world correctness.
nodes.why_it_matters: Subsystem convenience can turn into wrong-lifetime global state and invisible god-service coupling.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Host semantics stable; exact initialise/deinitialise dependencies need API/source check.

hands_on_tasks: Compare hidden lookup with explicit composition-boundary injection across two Worlds

nodes.name: Gameplay Transactions, Revisions, and Persistence
nodes.summary: Design authoritative commands, read-only queries, committed deltas/revisions and versioned stable-ID records for inventory, quests, interaction and save/load.
nodes.why_it_matters: Duplication, stale UI, missed objectives and broken saves are protocol/identity failures more often than container-choice failures.

version_notes.label: Stable architecture; UE5.3–UE5.6 implementation
version_notes.details: Fast Array, replicated subobject and platform save APIs require dedicated version depth.

hands_on_tasks: Implement revisioned inventory, late-activated quest and v1-to-v2 save migration

nodes.name: Hard/Soft References and Dependency Closure
nodes.summary: Choose hard, soft-class/object and weak references from identity, co-residency and lifetime needs while auditing transitive dependency closure.
nodes.why_it_matters: A small root can accidentally load large content graphs, while soft references alone do not solve cook or residency.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Core semantics stable; auditing/loading tooling evolves.

hands_on_tasks: Compare hard and soft definition closure and residency

nodes.name: Async Asset Request and Residency Lifecycle
nodes.summary: Scope asynchronous loads with handles/generations, weak owners, cancellation/failure states and explicit post-completion residency.
nodes.why_it_matters: Late callbacks, replaced requests, travel and released temporary ownership produce stale updates and unloaded assets.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Handle, cancellation and loader internals are branch-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Close/travel/reorder requests and verify safe cancellation/commit

nodes.name: Asset Registry, Asset Manager, Primary Assets, and Bundles
nodes.summary: Use Registry metadata for unloaded discovery and Asset Manager Primary identity/rules/bundles for managed loading and cook policy.
nodes.why_it_matters: Discovery, identity, loading and cook management are distinct responsibilities often conflated in weak designs.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Scan/rule/bundle APIs and audit UI evolve.

hands_on_tasks: Query metadata without load and test UI/Game Primary bundles

nodes.name: Cooking, Packaging, Chunks, Redirectors, and DDC
nodes.summary: Separate build/cook/stage/package/deploy and diagnose target inclusion, chunks, moves and derived-data effects from logs/manifests.
nodes.why_it_matters: Editor success hides uncooked assets, stale paths, platform formats and cold derived-data failures.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6/platform-sensitive
version_notes.details: Archive, cooker, IoStore and DDC/Zen pipelines evolve.

hands_on_tasks: Diagnose intentionally uncooked content and repair explicit management
source_conflicts: Maintained packaging docs can describe later archive defaults.

nodes.name: Level and World Partition Streaming Lifetime
nodes.summary: Design streaming sources/readiness, spatial loading, cross-cell references and durable state/async cleanup across world regions.
nodes.why_it_matters: Unloaded Actors, teleport races and cross-cell dependencies cause correctness failures beyond simple memory optimisation.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: World Partition hash/settings/commands are version-sensitive; World Composition is legacy.

hands_on_tasks: Preload teleport destination and test unload-safe persistent state

nodes.name: World Partition Grid, Streaming Sources, and Spatial Actor Policy
nodes.summary: Design grid/source/readiness policy, spatial versus always-loaded Actors and cross-cell reference constraints for large UE5 worlds.
nodes.why_it_matters: World Partition automates cell streaming but does not decide durable state, teleport readiness, reference hygiene or target performance for the project.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 target; runtime hash sensitive
version_notes.details: Grid/runtime hash settings, debug views and streaming-source APIs require target-branch verification.

common_bugs: Durable state stored only on spatial Actors
Hard reference keeps a region loaded
Multiple runtime grids added without measurement
hands_on_tasks: Implement destination streaming-source preload and fast-travel readiness proof
interview_questions: How would you design a UE5 open-world production pipeline?
How do you prevent fast travel into unloaded space?

nodes.name: Runtime Data Layers and Durable World State
nodes.summary: Use Runtime Data Layers to present phase/event/progression state while durable truth remains in save, quest or server-owned systems.
nodes.why_it_matters: Layer state can make a world look correct while save/load, late join, replication or content migration is still broken.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 target; API sensitive
version_notes.details: Data Layer subsystem and runtime-state APIs must be compiled against the target branch.

common_bugs: Data Layer treated as save authority
Late join sees wrong world phase
hands_on_tasks: Drive two Runtime Data Layers from a saveable world-state coordinator
interview_questions: How should Runtime Data Layers be used for progression?

nodes.name: OFPA, Level Instances, and Reusable POI Collaboration
nodes.summary: Use OFPA to reduce edit conflicts and choose Level Instances or Packed Level Blueprints from reuse, density, runtime mode and identity needs.
nodes.why_it_matters: Large-world authoring fails when source-control storage, reusable POIs and runtime gameplay identity are treated as the same problem.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 target; editor workflow sensitive
version_notes.details: OFPA changelist tooling and Level Instance runtime modes require branch/project verification.

common_bugs: Encoded files reviewed without Actor mapping
Non-OFPA actor lost in embedded mode
hands_on_tasks: Create one Level Instance POI and one static packed visual cluster, then validate packaged output
interview_questions: What can go wrong with One File Per Actor?
Level Instance or Packed Level Blueprint?

nodes.name: World Partition HLOD Build and Validation
nodes.summary: Choose HLOD layers/proxy settings from the measured cost, build proxies in automation and validate far/transition/near behavior in packaged traversal.
nodes.why_it_matters: HLOD can hide distant cost but cannot repair near-field activation, collision, material artefacts, stale build output or streaming hitches.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 target; content sensitive
version_notes.details: HLOD settings, builder commandlets, debug views and proxy output require target verification.

common_bugs: Near-field activation hitch hidden by proxy
hands_on_tasks: Build HLOD for a region and capture far/transition/near performance and artefacts
interview_questions: How do you evaluate HLOD for a large world?
What belongs in a packaged large-world validation gate?

nodes.name: PCG Graphs, Points, Density, and Attributes
nodes.summary: Model PCG as spatial data flowing from a Component through graph nodes into points with density, metadata domains and generated output.
nodes.why_it_matters: Procedural tools become unshippable when point data, density, attributes and generated ownership are treated as random scatter.

version_notes.label: UE5 plugin/runtime-sensitive
version_notes.details: PCG plugin APIs, node fields and runtime support must be verified in target branch.

hands_on_tasks: Build a parameterised biome graph with inspected point attributes

nodes.name: PCG Generation Modes and Output Ownership
nodes.summary: Choose editor-only, editor-generated, partitioned/hierarchical or runtime PCG with explicit output cleanup, streaming, save/load and authority policy.
nodes.why_it_matters: Runtime generation promises often fail from hitches, duplicated output, cooked-reference gaps, World Partition mismatches or unowned gameplay state.

version_notes.label: UE5 plugin/runtime-sensitive
version_notes.details: Generation modes and World Partition integration require target tests.

common_bugs: Generated gameplay state has no save identity
hands_on_tasks: Compare editor-generated and runtime PCG with cleanup and packaged proof
interview_questions: When should PCG run at runtime?

nodes.name: PCG Debugging and Reproducibility
nodes.summary: Freeze seed, graph version, inputs and parameters, then inspect point counts, density, transforms, attributes and node phases to find the first contradiction.
nodes.why_it_matters: Procedural bugs cannot be fixed reliably without reproducible inputs and phase-by-phase evidence.

version_notes.label: UE5 plugin/editor-sensitive
version_notes.details: Debug UI and non-shipping debug node behavior require target verification.

common_bugs: Changing density before finding bad phase
hands_on_tasks: Diagnose wrong output from attribute-domain mismatch and duplicate regeneration

nodes.name: PCG Performance and Generated Output Cost
nodes.summary: Separate graph execution cost from generated Actor/instance/render/collision/nav/streaming/memory cost and prove runtime generation on target hardware.
nodes.why_it_matters: PCG is often blamed for expensive output, while the real problem is spawned representation, collision, lighting, streaming or runtime hitches.

version_notes.label: UE5 plugin/platform-sensitive
version_notes.details: Runtime cost depends on platform, generated content and build configuration.

common_bugs: Graph time conflated with render cost
Runtime generation not profiled in packaged build
hands_on_tasks: Run 1x/10x/100x output scale and separate graph time from output cost

nodes.name: UBT, UHT, Compiler, Linker, and Load Pipeline
nodes.summary: Classify UBT graph construction, UHT generation, native compilation, linking, module loading and package stages from their evidence.
nodes.why_it_matters: Build failures persist when unrelated stages are conflated and cascading errors are treated as independent causes.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Rule properties, tool commands and generated output evolve.

common_bugs: Link error treated as include issue
hands_on_tasks: Trigger and classify UHT, compile, link and module-load failures
interview_questions: What do UBT, UHT, compiler and linker do?

nodes.name: Module Public/Private Dependency and API Boundaries
nodes.summary: Design Target/ModuleRules, self-contained public headers, private implementation, exports and IWYU/non-unity correctness.
nodes.why_it_matters: Transitive includes and public dependency fan-out create brittle builds, unresolved symbols and slow iteration.

version_notes.label: Stable module principles; UE5.3–UE5.6 rules
version_notes.details: Exact ModuleRules/TargetRules properties evolve.

hands_on_tasks: Repair transitive include, missing export and public dependency fan-out

nodes.name: Plugin Runtime/Editor Modules and Load Boundaries
nodes.summary: Keep runtime schemas/package targets independent from editor extensions while declaring plugin module hosts, content and legitimate load phases.
nodes.why_it_matters: Editor-only dependency leakage is a common reason clean Game/Server/Shipping builds and packaged content fail.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Descriptor schema, host types and loading phases are version-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Build two-module plugin and prove Editor exclusion in package
source_conflicts: Some maintained plugin examples use older module-type terminology.

nodes.name: Live Coding, Reinstancing, and Clean-Restart Boundaries
nodes.summary: Use binary patching for iteration while treating reflected layout, defaults/subobjects, build graph and static lifetime as restart/rebuild proof boundaries.
nodes.why_it_matters: Stale UObject/static caches and old/new destructor/layout state can corrupt assets or crash long after a seemingly successful patch.

version_notes.label: UE5.6 target
version_notes.details: Live Coding availability/settings and reinstancing behaviour are target/version-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Compare function, reflected layout, constructor and static cache changes live versus clean

nodes.name: Editor Automation Surfaces and Safe Batch Tools
nodes.summary: Choose Utility Widget, Python, C++ extension or commandlet and share a dry-run/transactional/idempotent operation core across UI and CI.
nodes.why_it_matters: Unsafe batch tools can corrupt thousands of assets faster than manual workflows and are difficult to recover without audit/source-control design.

version_notes.label: UE5.3–UE5.6 preferred
version_notes.details: Utility Widget/Python/editor APIs are Beta/Experimental or version-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Expose one bounded audit/repair core through Editor UI and commandlet

nodes.name: Asset Data Validation and Explicit Repair
nodes.summary: Run deterministic side-effect-free asset invariants on save/manual/CI and keep repair in separately previewed transactional tools.
nodes.why_it_matters: Early actionable content failures prevent runtime/cook regressions while silent validator mutation makes CI and source control unsafe.

version_notes.label: UE5.6 target
version_notes.details: Blueprint/Python validator discovery and Python API are plugin/version-sensitive.

hands_on_tasks: Add stable ID/reference/value validators plus separate idempotent fix action

nodes.name: AutomationTool, BuildGraph, and Release Gates
nodes.summary: Model release automation as explicit build, cook, stage/package, validation, smoke and artifact graph operations rather than editor-local success.
nodes.why_it_matters: A release pipeline must prove reproducibility, dependency order, artifact ownership and clean-machine behaviour before distribution.

version_notes.label: UE5.6 target; platform/build-farm sensitive
version_notes.details: BuildGraph tasks, RunUAT flags and CI wrappers must be checked against target engine and studio farm.

common_bugs: One long shell script hides dependencies
Broad cook setting masks missing rules
hands_on_tasks: Create a parameterised RunUAT or BuildGraph pipeline with explicit build/cook/package/smoke artifacts

nodes.name: Release Artifacts, Symbols, and Crash Proof
nodes.summary: Archive packaged output, logs, manifests, metadata and symbols together, then prove crash reporting/symbolication in a packaged build.
nodes.why_it_matters: Production crashes without exact symbols and build metadata are slow or impossible to diagnose.

version_notes.label: UE5.6 target; operations/privacy sensitive
version_notes.details: Crash endpoint, symbol upload, report routing and platform crash flows are project/studio policy decisions.

common_bugs: Log tail or custom context missing
hands_on_tasks: Force a packaged crash and verify symbolicated stack plus exact archived artifact mapping
interview_questions: How fit crash reporting into release?
Packaged build crashes but editor works?

nodes.name: CI Matrix and Clean-Machine Packaging Proof
nodes.summary: Split CI into fast pre-submit, nightly clean, release-candidate and platform/certification tiers with explicit runtime, artifacts and owners.
nodes.why_it_matters: A practical CI matrix catches editor-local assumptions without making every commit pay full release cost.

version_notes.label: Studio/platform-sensitive
version_notes.details: Hardware availability, platform SDKs, signing/certification and farm policy determine the final matrix.

common_bugs: All platforms run on every submit and get bypassed
Third-party runtime dependency only present locally
hands_on_tasks: Design a four-tier CI matrix with runtime budgets, artifact outputs and release blockers

nodes.name: Mobile Constraints, Device Profiles, and Scalability Proof
nodes.summary: Prepare mobile/device-tier builds with explicit budgets, device profiles, scalability tiers, mobile rendering policy and target-device evidence.
nodes.why_it_matters: Mobile and constrained platforms fail from thermal, memory, toolchain, renderer and lifecycle realities that editor testing hides.

version_notes.label: Platform/toolchain-sensitive
version_notes.details: Android/iOS SDK, NDK, Xcode, provisioning, package and renderer support change over time.

hands_on_tasks: Run two-tier mobile/platform packaged proof with profile and scalability evidence

nodes.name: Platform Release Artifact and Toolchain Contract
nodes.summary: Archive exact package, symbols, manifests, logs, metadata, toolchain versions and command parameters for clean-device diagnosis and release proof.
nodes.why_it_matters: Device-only failures and certification issues cannot be diagnosed if the package, symbols, manifests or build commands are not reproducible.

version_notes.label: Platform/store-sensitive
version_notes.details: Android/iOS/console package/signing/store details are target-sensitive; console docs may be confidential.

common_bugs: Package launches only on dev machine
hands_on_tasks: Archive one packaged platform build and prove forced crash symbolication

nodes.name: Platform Lifecycle and Certification-Adjacent Matrix
nodes.summary: Convert platform lifecycle and certification categories into expected behavior, evidence, owner and risk rows without leaking or inventing confidential requirements.
nodes.why_it_matters: Certification failures often come from unhandled suspend/resume, input, storage, account, network, entitlement, overlay or crash states.

version_notes.label: Source-sensitive/platform-confidential
version_notes.details: Exact console TRC/TCR/XR requirements require authorised platform-holder documentation.

hands_on_tasks: Create a certification-adjacent matrix with expected/observed/evidence/owner for five failures
source_conflicts: Exact certification text is not public and must come from platform-holder docs.

nodes.name: Platform Target Profiling and Release Gate
nodes.summary: Measure packaged target performance over representative and thermal-length sessions, then gate with percentiles, hitches, memory, I/O and artifact evidence.
nodes.why_it_matters: A build can pass desktop/editor performance gates and still fail target devices from thermals, memory, I/O, package settings or platform profiler findings.

version_notes.label: Platform/hardware-sensitive
